          1. Nansen Launches AI Cryptocurrency Trading Tool

          According to Jinse Finance, Nansen has launched its own cryptocurrency trading tool, enabling users to execute trades through an AI agent and natural language prompts. The platform's functionality has expanded from analysis to trade execution. Nansen announced on Wednesday that this new feature allows users to trade without using traditional charts or order books by entering conversational instructions in its mobile application. The product aims to serve retail users seeking a more convenient channel for trading in the cryptocurrency market. Nansen AI can also analyze on-chain signals, providing data-driven insights to guide investor decisions, and then execute trades in what the platform calls "intuitive trading." The company describes this approach as combining analytics with automation while retaining ultimate control in the hands of the user. According to Jinse Finance, Vitalik Buterin stated that he plans to fully return to decentralized social networks in 2026, believing that a better society requires better large-scale communication tools. These tools should help people filter high-quality information and viewpoints, find consensus, and serve the long-term interests of users, rather than maximizing short-term interactions and emotional conflict. Vitalik points out that there is no "one-size-fits-all" solution to these problems, but enhancing competition is an important starting point, and decentralization is the key path to achieving competition: through a shared data layer, anyone can build different clients on top of it. He revealed that since the beginning of this year, he has been using decentralized social tools for reading and posting, with all content synchronized across platforms such as X, Lens, Farcaster, and Bluesky via Firefly. He also criticized some crypto social projects for deviating from their original purpose, excessively viewing "token issuance" as innovation and attempting to create price bubbles around individuals to incentivize creators. However, practice shows that such models often reward existing social capital rather than content quality, and the tokens have short lifespans. Vitalik emphasized that money and social interaction are not inherently conflicting; the key lies in whether it truly serves the content itself, such as a subscription-based support model, rather than a speculative asset design. In his view, decentralized social interaction should be driven by teams that genuinely care about the "essence of social interaction." Vitalik acknowledged the Aave team's long-term maintenance of Lens and expressed anticipation for the direction of the new team, believing they are more focused on solving real social problems. He stated that in the coming year, he will speak out more on decentralized social platforms and encourage more users to participate in ecosystems like Lens and Farcaster, moving beyond the information-confrontational environment of a single platform and exploring new forms of interaction.

          3. The Kingdom of Bhutan plans to deploy and run the Sei Network validator in the first quarter.

          According to Jinse Finance, the Kingdom of Bhutan plans to deploy and run the Sei Network validator in the first quarter. The validator will be launched through a collaboration between the Sei Development Foundation and the technology department of Drug Holding and Investments (DHI)—Bhutan's main sovereign wealth fund and holding company. Phuntsho Namgay, Head of Innovation and Technology at DHI, stated that as part of its digital transformation goals, the wealth fund plans to continue exploring other collaboration opportunities with the Sei Development Foundation.

          ... "This collaboration marks an exciting step forward for Bhutan in strengthening its role in global blockchain innovation, while also opening new avenues for data valuation, scientific advancement, and fintech." 4. Solana Mobile's native token SKR is now listed on Jupiter. According to Jinse Finance, Jupiter announced on its X platform that Solana Mobile's native token SKR is now listed on Jupiter. It can be traded this week on Jupiter's web, mobile, and desktop platforms. 5. Grayscale applies to convert its Near Trust into an ETF. According to Jinse Finance, Grayscale has filed an S-1 document with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, applying to convert the Grayscale Near Trust into a Grayscale Near Trust ETF.

            Disclaimer: Jinse Finance, as a blockchain information platform, publishes articles for informational purposes only and does not constitute actual investment advice. Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: The inevitable trend of the rise of equity tokens
data.description:
Author: MattyTranslator: Jiahuan, ChainCatcher

In November 2025, more than 5 years after the $UNI airdrop, Uniswap finally activated its fee switch.

This process was delayed for several years and involved repeated governance battles, even witnessing an extremely embarrassing scene in 2024: a 'stakeholder' (widely believed to be an equity investor) blocked a proposal that should have rewarded token holders. Despite this, the UNIFICation proposal ultimately passed with over 62 million votes.

The fact that Uniswap, as the largest DEX in the crypto space, has taken so long to figure out how to reward its token holders speaks volumes about the current state of equity versus token relations. While UNI token holders theoretically "own" the protocol, they can only watch from the sidelines as equity investors reap all the value from front-end fees. While Uniswap is a prime example of this equity versus token divergence, this problem has been festering for years, affecting virtually every protocol that consistently generates revenue. Equity holders and token holders frequently compete for the same value pool while operating under fundamentally different legal, governance, and economic frameworks. The solutions proposed within the industry are varied: from completely eliminating equity and transferring all ownership on-chain to going to the other extreme—completely abandoning tokens. Both approaches have their supporters, but also significant drawbacks. Extreme Path One: Complete De-equityization Completely eliminating equity and transferring all ownership concepts on-chain is undoubtedly a theoretical solution. In this vision, smart contracts replace shareholder agreements, on-chain balances replace equity structure tables, and governance tokens replace board voting. Instant settlement. Transparent ownership. What's not to like? A major problem is: unless a company's assets, operations, and customers are entirely on-chain, off-chain court systems will always be the ultimate arbitrators in resolving disputes. You can try to make all your off-chain contracts and protocols reference on-chain logic, but this still doesn't change the fact that off-chain courts are arbitrators, and not everything is within your control and can be transferred to on-chain. For example, I could have a tokenized real estate NFT issued by a smart contract claiming ownership of the property, but if the off-chain title deed for that land differs, good luck presenting your NFT when the sheriff delivers the eviction notice. (Again, you can take steps to try and ensure that the off-chain title deed matches the on-chain state, but this doesn't negate the fact that off-chain enforcement has priority.) The "no equity, pure token" approach only applies to a small subset of projects: Completely on-chain networks and protocols, such as Bitcoin, some public chains, and fully autonomous DeFi. These projects have no companies, no employees, no servers, and no external dependencies. After all, that's the beauty of Bitcoin in its original form! An uncensorable system and unconfiscable assets. However, this is not feasible for the vast majority of projects (and the vast majority of potential on-chain activity). Web2 and Web2.5 companies own off-chain assets, customers, payments, and operations. Extreme Path Two: Complete Detoxification At the other extreme of the spectrum, some projects (in fact, the vast majority of companies) have decided to completely abandon tokens. They raise equity, build products, and avoid all the headaches that tokens can bring—while sacrificing all the benefits. Benefits: No tokens mean no SEC knocking on your door. No need to worry about whether governance tokens are securities. No need to design token economics, worry about emissions, or explain buyback mechanisms. Costs: Giving up instant settlement, transparent ownership records, cost-efficiency benefits, and the ability to coordinate global community incentives. Traditional equity transfers are expensive, slow to settle, and inaccessible to most potential investors. Gaining equity exposure in private startups remains expensive, inefficient, and opaque. Even in 2026, the processes required to trade public stocks will seem outdated compared to DeFi. Tokens, despite their shortcomings, have the potential to solve these problems. They enable community ownership and user-owned products. To abandon this entirely would be a step backward. To find the optimal balance between these two extremes, we need to understand what equity offers that tokens cannot. What do equity and tokens each offer? 1. Legal Rights and Recourse When you own equity, you have legal standing. You can sue and enforce your rights. If directors breach fiduciary duty or commit fraud, you have an established legal framework to recover your losses. Token holders (with very few exceptions) have virtually no legally recognized rights or protections. They often have to rely on the market to save their investments. While theoretically a company's entire budget can be placed on-chain, allowing founders to delegate every decision to shareholders without legal authorization introduces significant operational inefficiencies and contradicts the initial purpose of investment—trusting the team's vision and capabilities. 2. Formal Governance Control Shareholders elect the board of directors, approve major transactions, and have the right to codify documents. In contrast, governance tokens often create an illusion of control. As Vitalik stated, token governance has serious flaws: low voting rates (<10%), whale manipulation, and a lack of expertise. More often than not, on-chain governance degenerates into a "decentralized theater," where teams can often ignore votes if they don't like the outcome, as execution still requires manual intervention. 3. Legal Clarity of Value Accumulation In mergers and acquisitions, equity holders have a clear legal right to receive profits. As recent cases with Tensor and Axelar have shown, token holders are often completely abandoned, even after the project has been acquired. Due to this strong legal right to profit sharing, stocks are more reliably traded based on multiples of expected future profits. Token valuations, on the other hand, are often purely speculative and lack fundamental support. Even if a project generates revenue, most projects do not reliably route that revenue to token holders due to regulatory risks and fiduciary duty conflicts. While off-chain protocols can be built to simulate this right, this is far less reliable than the legal basis of equity. 4. A Broader and Deeper Investor Pool In short, the equity market has a much larger investor pool and greater total purchasing power than the token market. The value of the US stock market alone is more than 20 times that of the entire crypto industry. The value of the global stock market is more than 46 times that of the crypto industry. Projects that choose tokens over equity are actually only reaching 2%-5% of the potential purchasing power they could otherwise access. 2026: The Year of Equity Tokens One thing is certain: from tokenized equity to new forms of on-chain governance, 2026 will be a year of innovation and experimentation with equity tokens. The DTC pilot project (launching in the second half of 2026) will be the first time in the US that participants are allowed to hold tokenized securities interests on a blockchain. This represents a shift of the backbone of the US capital market infrastructure onto the blockchain: Nasdaq has proposed trading tokenized securities. Securitize offers real publicly traded shares with full on-chain legal ownership. Centrifuge and others are tokenizing equity through SEC-registered agents. The integration of traditional financial infrastructure with the blockchain track is no longer a pipe dream—it's happening. For crypto-native projects, Uniswap's five-year journey toward a fee switch serves as a warning. The equity vs. token split won't resolve itself automatically. It requires deliberate design, clear protocols, and structures to resolve conflicts of interest. Ultimately, this divergence stems from regulatory uncertainty and a lack of a legal framework. Whether through the SEC's "Crypto Projects" or the Clarity Act, the US is expected to achieve long-awaited regulatory certainty as early as January. By the end of this year, we will no longer be discussing equity vs. tokens. We will be discussing ownership—transparent, transferable, legally protected, and natively digital ownership.

data.title: Predicting the Market 2025: Hype, Risks, and Regulatory Maneuvering
data.description:
Author: D50 Source: X, @Kristintt

In 2025, prediction markets were simultaneously attracting massive amounts of capital and frequently encountering problems, pushed to an unprecedented crossroads amidst the tug-of-war between hype, risk, and regulation.

Foreword:

If you only started paying attention to prediction markets in 2026, you'll likely have this feeling: prediction markets are so big, changing so fast, and so chaotic. In the two weeks since I started writing this article, the market has seen a series of events, including controversial transactions and reversals, platforms starting to charge transaction fees, old projects issuing new tokens, and new regulatory bills being debated again—I've found that there's no way to cover them all.

These changes didn't just happen in 2026; they're more like the result of continuous accumulation throughout 2025, exploding in the new year. In 2025, the prediction market experienced a true period of expansion. However, at the same time, the pressure on infrastructure, the blurring of regulatory boundaries, and the divergence in regulatory attitudes remained largely unaddressed. Therefore, this article won't chase after the new stories that have already unfolded in 2026, but instead chooses to return to 2025 and re-examine how the prediction market has arrived at its current state in just one year. Understanding 2025 might help you understand why the prediction market "has become what it is" in 2026.

Table of Contents

  • Market Overview

  • Main Projects

  • Main Mechanisms of Prediction Markets

  • User Gameplay

  • Ecosystem Building

  • Risks, Regulation, and Black Swans

The main text begins below. This article contains approximately 8.6k words (reading time approximately 30 minutes)

Figure - Forecast market (nominal) trading volume in 2025

In 2025, Wall Street capital entered Polymarket, and Kalshi also achieved rapid growth by leveraging its compliance advantages and multiple collaborations. Although the overall market still exhibits a duopoly, prediction market projects in other ecosystems and sectors have also emerged and achieved remarkable results in their respective sub-sectors.

...

Figure - 2025 Prediction Market Trading Volume Share

As the political prediction frenzy triggered by last year's US election gradually subsided, the sports market experienced a boom in 2025, propelling Kalshi to successfully reverse its previous market position. The changes in the two leading prediction market platforms show that 2025 became a crucial year for sports and political betting to dominate the market rhythm.

...

Figure - Polymarket and Kalshi Open Market Types

2. Major Projects

Entering 2025, in addition to significant changes in the original two major prediction markets, prediction market projects in other sub-sectors have also emerged in different forms. The following is a brief overview of the major prediction market projects that have launched their mainnets in 2025.

2.1 Polymarket: A Wall Street Newcomer, Still the Leader in On-Chain Prediction Markets

Polymarket is currently the undisputed leader in on-chain prediction markets. The platform primarily predicts the outcomes of various events. It first launched its mainnet on Polygon in 2020 and now supports more public chains and mainstream tokens for trading. Polymarket secured $150 million in funding in early 2025, but its most notable achievement was the $2 billion investment from ICE, the parent company of the NYSE, in October. This $2 billion check directly boosted Polymarket's valuation from $1.2 billion to $9 billion. From then on, Polymarket leaped from the gray market to a Wall Street newcomer, igniting the ecosystem's momentum in the prediction market in the second half of the year.

Following the high level of attention brought by the 2024 US presidential election, compliance pressures and market coverage have led Polymarket to shift its focus towards sports and other broader thematic markets. It has also partnered with Onyx Odds, the fastest-growing social sports prediction platform in the US, and UFC, the world's leading combat sports brand, to further implement this strategic plan. Currently, Polymarket's platform offers a diverse range of markets, but the largest share of trading volume remains largely related to political elections, followed by sports and cryptocurrencies. On the regulatory front, after acquiring the regulated exchange GCX and obtaining a license, Polymarket received good news at the end of 2025 – the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) finally ended its four-year regulatory restrictions, allowing it to return to the US market. Furthermore, Polymarket is also an official prediction market partner of X. Various investments and partnerships added a significant advantage to Polymarket in 2025, further solidifying its leading position in the on-chain prediction market. 2.2 Kalshi: A Magnificent Comeback Through Regulation and the Sports Market While Kalshi and Polymarket offer similar products, they follow different paths. Firstly, Kalshi's user base is limited to US users, and its payment methods are more traditional (e.g., debit cards, wire transfers). It also supports crypto payments, but its asset selection is far less diverse than Polymarket's. However, as an event-based prediction market that is not on-chain but has passed CFTC compliance regulations, Kalshi also possesses unique advantages. With Polymarket dominating the prediction market, Kalshi leveraged this advantage to stage a remarkable comeback: before 2025, Kalshi was far inferior to Polymarket in terms of both funding and transaction data. However, in 2025, it secured approximately $1.5 billion in funding through three rounds from established US VCs such as Paradigm, a16z, and Sequoia Capital, achieving a valuation of $11 billion, making it the highest-valued prediction market at the time. Another turning point occurred in March 2025. Like Polymarket, Kalshi recognized the enormous market potential of sports. It seized the opportunity presented by the March Madness tournament, the "elimination playoffs" of college basketball. Held annually in March, March Madness is one of the most popular annual events in the US, known for its single-elimination format, frequent upsets, and widespread betting. With its regulatory advantages and partnership with Robinhood, Kalshi amassed the largest betting community for March Madness. In March 2025, Kalshi achieved a fourfold increase in trading volume compared to the previous month. Following the "Crazy March," Kalshi reaped the benefits of the sports market, and through its continued partnership with Robinhood, a mainstream investment app for US retail investors, its trading momentum surged. In September of the same year, the NFL season began, and Kalshi attracted $441 million in trading volume during the opening week, continuously breaking daily trading volume records. This marked the first time Kalshi surpassed Polymarket in monthly trading volume, a significant turning point. For the next three months, Kalshi's platform trading volume consistently outpaced Polymarket, achieving a double harvest in both funding volume and platform trading volume in 2025.

Graph - Kalshi Transaction Volume and Tag Type Distribution

2.3 Limitless: A Short-Term High-Frequency Trading Player that Issues Tokens First

Besides Polymarket and Kalshi, the two leading prediction markets, Limitless, as the third largest, also achieved considerable results in 2025.

Chart - Limitless Monthly Trading Trends

2.4 Myriad: A Natural Flow Track Embedded in Media and Wallets

As the first native prediction market product built into a wallet in partnership with Trustwallet, it currently supports the Abstract, Linea, and BNB ecosystems.

Chart - Myriad Monthly Transaction Volume Trend

2.5 Football.Fun: Rekindling the Dawn of Gamefi in Prediction Markets

Football.Fun also takes a different approach, combining sports-related Gamefi with prediction markets based on the Base chain.

Football.Fun also takes a different approach, combining sports-related Gamefi with prediction markets based on the Base chain.

The platform's main gameplay involves obtaining virtual players through a gacha system. These virtual players then form teams and automatically participate in matches, with players profiting from the final results by holding onto these players. This gameplay is relatively rare in Chinese-speaking regions but is very popular in the US market. In the early days of sports betting in the US, it was a highly popular gray area industry, known as "Fantasy Sports." The platform currently offers free and professional versions, both with identical gameplay mechanics, but the former uses platform points while the latter uses real money. In July 2025, Football.Fun secured $2 million in funding from VCs such as 6th Man Ventures and Sfermion, and quickly completed a token sale exceeding $10 million in December, but did not achieve a TGE (Trusted Token Expiration) in 2025 (Note: It was TGEed on January 15, 2026). Compared to the lack of mention in the Chinese-speaking world, Football.Fun has gained significant attention in the European and American markets. When it was launched in early August, its actual monthly trading volume once surged to nearly $40 million, briefly surpassing Limitless. However, its popularity subsequently declined, and it has remained in a lukewarm state.

Chart - Football.Fun Monthly (Nominal) Trading Volume Trend

2.6 Opinion: Firing the First Shot in the BSC Prediction Market

Opinion, as the first BNB ecosystem prediction market project incubated by YZi Labs in 2024, leverages 2025 Binance's focus on prediction markets in 2016 not only garnered significant attention in the Chinese-speaking world but also led to a successful $5 million funding round from institutions such as YZi Labs, Amber Group, and echi. At the end of the year, Binance announced the completion of a new round of funding worth tens of millions of dollars. While both Opinion and Polymarket are event-driven prediction markets, Opinion employs a more crypto-native trading incentive approach. After launching its mainnet in October, Opinion initially used an invitation code system before opening full access to the public, simultaneously introducing rebates and points programs. This enabled Opinion to achieve a remarkable monthly trading volume exceeding 100 million in its first month, and it has continued to break new records since.

Chart - Opinion Monthly Trading Volume Trend

2.7 Predict.fun: A Profitable Prediction Market Embraced by Binance

Predict.fun is a prediction market that uses "predicting funds while generating returns" as its selling point. It was already established in September 2024.

Layer 2 Blast, focusing on native yields, launched its mainnet. The project's founder is a former Binance executive who, despite leaving due to negative controversies, showed signs of improvement in his relationship with CZ in 2025. In December of the same year, CZ announced the official launch of Predct.fun on the BNB Chain, making it another prediction market project in the BNB ecosystem. Predct.fun also adopted a points system after its launch, achieving over $70 million in initial trading volume in December. 3. Key Mechanisms of Prediction Markets 3.1 Oracles Although most prediction markets are currently event-driven, the oracles used vary.

Figure - Oracle Mechanisms Used in Mainstream Markets

As a true on-chain prediction market, unlike Kalshi's centralized team auditing approach, Polymarket primarily uses the UMA Optimistic Oracle and upgraded from OOv2 to MOOv2 in August 2025. In September, it introduced Chainlink as its primary data source.

The basic mechanism of UMA is as follows: an oracle bot submits an answer, which is initially considered correct. If anyone questions it, arbitration is conducted; otherwise, settlement takes place. The MOOv2 version adds a customization and management module compared to the OOv2 version, supporting the setting of parameters such as permissions and order book deposits. In the settlement process, DVM is UMA's decentralized adjudication system. UMA token holders can participate in voting and influence the final result (which has thus become one of the methods of malicious prediction market manipulation, as will be discussed later). The entire settlement process of UMA can be simplified as shown in the following diagram:

Diagram - Optimistic Oracle Settlement and DVM Arbitration Process

Another oracle used by Polymarket, Chainlink—as the head oracle—primarily uses multiple nodes to verify and transmit data. Each node obtains information from different data sources, and finally aggregates this data to arrive at the final result. Currently, it is mainly used to improve the efficiency of Polymarket's data platform services.

3.2 Order Book

Most prediction markets currently use the CLOB (Central Limit Order Book) trading model, which is a trading mechanism that centralizes all buy and sell orders in a single order book for matching and execution. This lays the foundation for order execution in prediction markets: in the central limit order book, traders can choose to place limit orders or market orders, and the system will match these buy and sell orders according to price and time priority.

To reduce latency while ensuring capital utilization, most prediction markets adopt an off-chain matching and on-chain settlement approach. Opinion is somewhat unique; based on its trust in the high performance of the BNB Chain, its orders are executed on-chain from matching to settlement, using a fully on-chain central limit order book.

Predict.fun uses a single order book model – specifically designed for binary prediction markets, displaying only buy and sell orders for "YES" while calculating the order book for "NO" in real time through mathematical complementarity. However, it also borrows from a central limit order book approach, using a unified matching logic. The overall order book is also managed through on-chain settlement and off-chain maintenance and matching. Meanwhile, a small number of prediction markets still use AMMs (Automated Market Makers). Initially, Polymarket used a similar AMM-like LMSR algorithm, but switched to a decentralized order book in 2025. This is because AMMs, widely used in DeFi, are not actually well-suited for prediction markets due to market structural issues.

Figure - Order Book Types Used in Mainstream Markets

4. User Play Methods

Currently, prediction markets are still in the exploratory stage, and various play methods are emerging. Here, we will take a landmark platform as an example and simply divide the current common play methods into three categories: directional trading, market-neutral strategies, and market maker profit strategies.

4.2 Market Neutral Strategy

The market neutral strategy breaks through the cognitive limitations of directional trading. Users do not need to understand the market background, but instead use various platform structures or time differences to profit from pricing deviations. (1) Sweeping the End of the Day As a high-probability trading strategy, sweeping the end of the day allows users to use a large amount of funds to buy when the market has high certainty—such as when the probability of an event is as high as 90+%. However, even with high certainty, a sudden market downturn may lead to serious losses of funds. (2) Structural Arbitrage Profits are made by using the structural problems of platforms and markets. This arbitrage method usually requires calculation using mathematical formulas. These arbitrage methods all use the same mathematical formula (the sum of the purchase costs of all outcomes is less than $1): Outcome(1) + Outcome(2) + ... + Outcome(n) < $1. The outcomes here must first satisfy a necessary condition: they are mutually exclusive and exhaustive (they cannot occur simultaneously, and one outcome must occur). Since you hold a position regardless of which outcome is ultimately determined, your final profit equals $1 minus the cost. The above inequality occurs in the following situations: a. Cross-platform arbitrage: The market for the same event may exist on different platforms, and the judgment results of these markets are usually consistent. Therefore, users can selectively purchase all results on different platforms to profit, provided that the sum of the purchase costs of each result is less than $1.

b. Cross-condition arbitrage: The same event may have multiple markets on the same platform, but the descriptions are not exactly the same. It can also be purchased at a price where the sum of the costs of all results is less than $1. c. Multi-result arbitrage: Commonly seen in election markets, the result YES usually has many options. If the sum of the purchase costs of all YES results is less than $1, it can also be purchased.

d. Single-result arbitrage: Commonly seen in price prediction markets, usually involving YES/NO predictions for the same price. If the cost of purchasing both YES and NO simultaneously is less than $1, a limit order can be used. (3) Triad market arbitrage: The triad market here mostly refers to the sports market, where there are only three possible outcomes: A wins, B wins, and a draw. Based on these three results, the price should conform to the following equation: A wins + draw = B wins. Therefore, when the market price shows such an inequality: A wins + draw < B wins, it can be concluded that the price of "B wins" is undervalued, and a buy order should be placed. (4) Price spread arbitrage The factors that cause price spreads are generally price imbalances caused by market making delays, hence it is also called "market making price spread imbalance arbitrage". This situation is often caused by problems such as poor liquidity, asymmetry between buy and sell orders, emotional rush to buy, and market maker price delays. The operation process is usually as follows: a. Find markets with large bid-ask spreads b. Use limit orders to buy at low prices c. Use limit orders to sell at high prices However, this arbitrage method requires users to have a high sense of market fluctuations and has a certain threshold. (5) Development of trading robots Trading robots based on prediction markets are currently a very popular direction in prediction markets. In the prediction market, which has high requirements for speed and information, the addition of robots is undoubtedly a great help to players a. Arbitrage robots As mentioned above, this arbitrage method composed of mathematical formulas is very suitable for robots to execute. Therefore, there are various arbitrage robots with different functions on the market, including binary market arbitrage robots, three-party market arbitrage robots, etc. b. Trend-following trading bots. These bots typically act opportunistically, providing liquidity to those experiencing price increases (actively placing sell orders to users chasing the rise) and buying at lower prices during periods of panic, waiting to sell after the next upward surge. c. LLM-based trading bots. When users lack market knowledge or sufficient information, LLM-based trading bots typically use data analysis to assist in making trading decisions. d. Copy trading bots. These bots track traders with high prediction success rates in the market and follow their trades. The selection criteria for specific traders are generally based on their equity curve, preferred markets, and profit/loss areas. e. Preemptive Robots The spread of information takes time. During the period from the occurrence of an event and its promotion to the start of order book submissions, robots can use automated processes to preempt ordinary manual trading users. Of course, these robots are also more susceptible to interference from fake news. 4.3 Market Maker Revenue Strategies As mentioned earlier, most prediction markets use a central limit order book trading model. Compared to AMMs, this trading model shifts market makers from a passive risk-bearing position to an active one. Except for platforms like Kalshi that cooperate with professional market maker institutions, the market maker role in most prediction markets is actively undertaken by users. Therefore, prediction markets also provide additional rewards to market makers who provide liquidity to the market through limit orders. Prediction markets like Polymarket distribute approximately $500,000 in trading volume per month. Becoming a market maker to earn profits also requires certain qualifications. First, users need to review the market's reward rules for market makers and filter the market accordingly. The market typically stipulates the minimum position size and the maximum allowed bid-ask spread for market makers to receive rewards. For market makers, the closer their limit orders are to the midpoint, the more generous the rewards will be. This reward mechanism exhibits a quadratic function characteristic—the further away from the midpoint, the faster the rewards diminish. Simultaneously, the system removes certain edge orders used to create "false midpoints" to prevent manipulation. Furthermore, market makers must consider the balance between risk and reward. The longer a market maker places an order, the more rewards accumulate, but longer periods also mean the potential for losses due to extreme market conditions. Discussions about arbitrage methods are raging in the market, from user-developed scripts to the emergence of professional arbitrage platforms and the commercial sale of arbitrage services. Participants are all trying to get a share of this seemingly complex game. However, due to Polymarket's use of an off-chain order book, neutral arbitrage is gradually becoming more of a tool. Given the limited order book depth in most markets, arbitrage competition is shifting from "discovering price differences" to "competing on execution speed." Simultaneously, market makers must constantly weigh their ultimate gains against platform subsidies and passive losses from placing orders. Under this structure, the arbitrage opportunities left for ordinary users are limited, and ultimately, only players with both rule understanding and risk management capabilities can truly benefit.

5. Ecosystem Building

As a platform centered on trading activity, the growth of prediction markets depends on continuously creating new trading demands and use cases. As a relatively mature representative of the ecosystem, Polymarket launched a Builder Program in 2025. This program encourages developers to build products around Polymarket, guiding more users to participate in trading, thereby driving the overall liquidity and trading volume of the platform, and providing corresponding incentives and support for outstanding builders.

Figure - Polymarket Builders Program Equity Table

Currently, Polymarket Builders projects are mainly divided into two categories: Trading Terminal and TG Bot.

Figure - Partial list of Polymarket Builders projects (ranked by trading volume)

These products mainly attempt to solve the current user experience and trading strategy problems of the Polymarket platform:

1. For users takers, Polymarket has a trading latency of approximately 500ms. This is an intentional anti-preemptive measure set by the platform. Additionally, the platform's servers located in Europe and London also experience some network latency.

In this situation, platform front-end still frequently experiences lag and data refresh delays. Therefore, most Trading Terminals and TG Bots optimize the user's order placement experience as a taker by leveraging official APIs and WebSocket RTDS real-time data sources, and simplifying order value settings. 2. In predictive markets, information advantage is crucial for directional trading. Therefore, many Trading Terminals use AI to capture real-time news related to market conditions, and users can use AI to access relevant materials or probability analysis to help them better understand market conditions and even make trades based on the information obtained. 3. Insider addresses, whales, and smart money are often highly watched trading entities in the market, and many products have also launched address tracking and copy trading functions. 4. In addition, there are some niche functions, such as spread detection for arbitrage users, predictive market ETFs, and multiple AI-analyzed market conditions. Through the analysis of these Polymarket builders' products, it's clear that prediction markets still face many user experience issues. However, this approach of incentivizing users to optimize their products not only provides existing users with a more comfortable trading experience but also attracts more new users with lower barriers to entry. The market's positive flywheel is leading more users into the ecosystem. 6. Risks, Regulation, and Black Swans 2025 was not a year of smooth development for prediction markets. Systemic shocks and unexpected events occurred one after another, such as platform outages, insider trading issues, and regulatory frictions, which continued to impact the market. Polymarket, as a leader, experienced multiple outages and service interruptions over its 25 years. Besides Polygon's underlying chain performance issues, subgraph data and the API search engine also frequently malfunctioned. Official data shows that Polymarket experienced frequent issues across its services in November. Server failures often resulted in losses for arbitrage users, and its infrastructure has long been criticized by the market. Furthermore, Polymarket also experienced a security incident in 2025 where a vulnerability in its external authentication provider allowed attackers to gain unauthorized access to some platform accounts; fortunately, only a small number of users were affected.

Chart - Polymarket November (2025) System Performance

Markets need to "create gods" to attract users, and prediction markets also provide retail investors with many tempting cases of making big profits with small investments. ... However, market data reveals a harsh reality: 70% of traders are losing money, while the top 0.04% of the remaining 30% who profit capture more than half of the 70% of profits. This concentration of profits at the top is approaching that of the perpetual contract market. The rampant insider trading and event manipulation are among the reasons for this concentration of profits. In October 2025, 12 hours before the Nobel Prize announcement, a newly registered user made a large, precise bet on the Peace Prize winner; shortly afterward, another trader heavily bet on an unpopular option in the Google search rankings and ultimately profited. Only afterward did ordinary players realize that the search rankings were generated based on Google's internal algorithm, and any slight adjustment to any parameter could change the results, completely invisible to external users. Compared to these insider trading practices, some manipulation tactics are even more abhorrent to ordinary users: Polymarket's DVM arbitration mechanism provides opportunities for malicious actors. As mentioned earlier, DVM arbitration is primarily conducted by UMA token holders, allowing UMA whales to use token voting to force decisions that contradict the truth. Although the official stance was ultimately that the decision was unfair, no compensation was offered to those who suffered losses. Insider trading and manipulation are not new in betting games, and various manipulation scandals are commonplace in the traditional gambling industry. However, crypto prediction markets conveniently provide these insiders/manipulators with a channel to conceal the source and destination of their funds. It is precisely for these reasons that the conflict between prediction markets and regulators is constant.

Figure - Prediction Markets and US Regulation in 2025

Prediction markets like Kalshi, which are already regulated by the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), will still experience significant friction with US regulators in 2025.

At the federal level, the CFTC classifies prediction markets as derivatives contracts/futures, allowing them to operate as long as they remain within the regulatory framework. However, at the state level, different states have their own regulatory agencies and laws. This two-tiered regulatory structure makes the US one of the most complex and prone to regulatory friction globally. Regulatory conflicts arise when some states claim that state laws take precedence over federal exemptions. Sports prediction markets are easily categorized as "gambling" by state regulators, and Kalshi's market is predominantly comprised of sports prediction markets, leading many states and agencies to file cease and desist orders against Kalshi. However, there is some good news on the regulatory front. Polymarket, after being accused by the CFTC of violating US futures laws in 2022, successfully returned to the US market in November 2025 through a shell company.

Figure - Global Regulatory Attitudes Towards Prediction Markets in 2025

Looking globally, European and Asian countries consistently hold varying degrees of prohibition towards prediction markets. Although prediction markets seem to be gradually being accepted by mainstream finance in 2025, their real challenge remains the insurmountable mountain of regulation.
